risk_of_bias.support_text: Comment: the authors only report that participants were randomly assigned.

Quote: “On completing the preparatory phase but prior to the patient beginning treatment, a research assistant consulted the randomisation table and notified the therapist of the patient’s treatment condition” (p 2651).

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel: All outcomes
risk_of_bias.support_text: Comment: not mentioned and unlikely to have been done
source_domains: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias): All outcomes

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of outcome assessment: All outcomes
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: “assessment was conducted by two psychologists blind to assignment” (p 2651)
source_domains: Blinding of outcome assessment (detection bias): All outcomes

risk_of_bias.support_text: Comment: the authors analysed data on an intention-to-treat (ITT) basis.
source_domains: Incomplete outcome data (attrition bias): All outcomes

characteristics.participants:
Inclusion criteria:“Inclusion criteria were female sex, age 13 to 18 years, and a primary diagnosis from the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (Fourth Edition, Text Revision) of chronic or subthreshold PTSD related to sexual abuse that occurred at least 3months prior to assessment for the study. Subthreshold PTSD was defined as: 1 or more re‐experiencing symptoms, 2 or more avoidance symptoms, 2 or more arousal symptoms, and a total score of 14 or greater on the Child PTSD Symptom Scale–Interview.” (p 2651)
Exclusion criteria:“current suicidal ideation with intent, uncontrolled bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, conduct disorder, pervasive developmental disorder, initiation of psychotropic medication within the previous 12 weeks, and current inpatient psychiatric treatment. Adolescents with substance use or suicidality without imminent threat were not excluded” (p 2651)
Gender:all participants were girls
Age:range = 7 to 13 years
Type of abuse:not reported
Onset of abuse:not reported
Length of abuse:not reported
Perpetrator of abuse:not reported
characteristics.interventions:
Participants (n = 61) randomly assigned to one of two groups


Cognitive behavioural therapy (classified as CBT; n = 31) 

Therapy format: individual

Duration of each session: 90 minutes

Caretaker included: no

Duration of intervention: 14 weeks

Child centred therapy (classified as CCT; n = 30) 

Therapy format: individual

Duration of each session: 90 minutes

Caretaker included: no

Duration of intervention: 14 weeks
characteristics.outcomes:
Time points for assessment:baseline, mid‐treatment, post‐treatment, 3‐month follow‐up, 6‐month follow‐up, and 12‐month follow‐up
Measures (outcomes):Child PTSD Symptom Scale–Interview (CPSS‐I; PTSD), Children’s Global Assessment Scale (CGAS; PTSD), Children Depression Inventory (CDI; depression), and Expectancy of Therapeutic Outcome for Adolescents (ETO‐A, satisfaction with treatment).
The following outcomes were used for the quantitative analyses:


post‐traumatic stress disorder

depression
characteristics.notes:
Start and end dates:no more information than "Data collection lasted from February 2006 through March 2012." (p 2650)
Funding sources:"This study was conducted with support from the National Institute of Mental Health (R01 MH074505) awarded to Dr Foa." (p 2656)
Study’s limitations as reported by the study's authors:“First, the pre‐randomization preparatory sessions may reduce generalizability to treatment studies in which participants are randomized immediately after the baseline evaluation. Second, the preparatory sessions occurred prior to randomization, which may have biased the sample by excluding inappropriate participants and including more motivated ones. Third, because the sample comprised female adolescents whose PTSD was related to sexual abuse, the results may not generalize to PTSD related to other types of traumas or to males” (p 2656)

sections.text:
Suicide is the second leading cause of death in adolescence (Centers for Disease Control & Prevention, 2015). Several factors may partially account for the high risk for suicide in adolescence, including interpersonal difficulties (Finkelhor, Ormrod & Turner, 2009), sleep disorders (Bernert, Kim, Iwata, & Perlis, 2015), depression (Franklin et al., 2017), and posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD; Brown, Armey, Sejourne, Miller, & Weinstock, 2016). The association between PTSD and suicide is especially important because of high rates of trauma exposure during adolescence (Mclaughlin et al., 2013).

Nearly two-thirds of adolescents report experiencing at least one potentially traumatic event (Mclaughlin et al., 2013). Reductions in parental supervision, increased sexual activity, and delinquent behavior each increase risk for personal victimization during adolescence (Finkelhor et al., 2009). Approximately 4.7% of adolescents develop PTSD following exposure to a traumatic event (Mclaughlin et al., 2013).

Compared to individuals with no history of trauma exposure, individuals with PTSD are more likely to report both suicidal thoughts (30–80%) and behaviors (15–50%; Panagioti, Gooding, Triantafyllou & Tarrier, 2015). The relationship between PTSD and suicide risk holds even after controlling for depression, violence exposure, age, gender, borderline personality disorder, panic disorder, obsessive compulsive disorder, and social phobia (Mazza, 2000).

Fortunately, several evidence-based practices have been validated for the treatment of PTSD in adolescents. One such treatment is prolonged exposure therapy for adolescents (PE-A; Foa, Chrestman, & Gilboa-Schechtman, 2008), which is rooted in emotional processing therapy (Foa & Kozak, 1985). PE-A resulted in significantly greater PTSD and depression symptom reductions relative to client-centered therapy (CCT; Foa, McLean, Capaldi, & Rosenfield, 2013) and psychodynamic psychotherapy (Gilboa-Schechtman et al., 2010). To our knowledge, no studies have investigated the efficacy of PE-A on suicide.

Clinician concern about the potential for exacerbation of suicide risk has largely prevented the use of trauma-focused treatments in patients with PTSD and suicide risk (Van Minnen, Zoellner, Garbed & Mills, 2015). To our knowledge no study has found that trauma-focused treatments were associated with worsened suicidal ideation (SI) in adults; in fact, PE was associated with significant reductions in SI in veterans (Cox et al., 2016), and civilians (Gradus et al., 2013). Furthermore, reduction in PTSD symptom severity preceded and partially explained reduction in suicidal thoughts in PE for adults (Cox et al., 2016), suggesting that PTSD symptom reduction may be the mechanism underlying improvement in SI in PE. However, other studies have also indicated that improvements in depression may also be an important mechanism of PTSD treatment response (Bryan et al., 2016). Regardless of the mechanisms of action, these findings suggest the importance of providing trauma-focused treatment in adults with PTSD and suicide risk. As no research has examined the effect of trauma-focused treatment on suicide outcomes in adolescents, no clinical recommendations are available for clinicians working with this population.

The current study compared the change in SI over time for adolescent females randomized to receive either PE-A or CCT for PTSD following a sexual assault (Foa et al., 2013). Participants were not recruited on the basis of SI, though 40% of the sample endorsed baseline SI. First, we aimed to determine whether SI changed significantly across both treatments. We hypothesized that there would be significant reductions in SI in both treatments. Second, we aimed to examine whether there were significant differences in the rate of change in SI or in overall SI between PE-A and CCT. We hypothesized that compared to CCT, PE-A would be associated with significantly faster and overall greater reduction in SI. Third, we aimed to examine whether the slope of PTSD and depression symptom improvement was associated with the slope of SI improvement in both groups. We hypothesized that steeper reductions in PTSD and depression symptoms would be associated with steeper reductions in SI.

## Participants

Participants (n = 61) were adolescent females (13–18 years old) recruited through a rape crisis center in Philadelphia, PA, an urban city in the United States. Inclusion criteria were: 1) a primary diagnosis of chronic or subthreshold PTSD (defined as only 2/3 avoidance symptoms and a total score ≥14 on the Child Posttraumatic Stress Scale-Interview, CPSS-I; Foa, Johnson, Feeny & Treadwell, 2001), and 2) sexual abuse index trauma at least 3 months earlier. Exclusion criteria included untreated bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, conduct disorder, pervasive developmental disorder, imminent suicide risk (SI with intent or SI with imminent plans), current inpatient psychiatric treatment, and initiation of psychotropic medication within the previous 12 weeks. At baseline, 39.3% of participants endorsed SI. Adolescents were included if they denied imminent threat to their safety and were not recruited on the basis of SI. Participants were randomized to CCT (n = 30) or PE-A (n = 31; see Table 1).

## Measures

### Child PTSD Symptom-Scale Interview (CPSS-I)

The CPSS-I (Foa et al., 2001) is a 24-item measure of PTSD symptom severity for ages 8 to 18 years (range: 0 – 51). The CPSS-I has excellent internal consistency (Cronbach α = .89) and test retest reliability (r = .84 Foa et al., 2001).

## Measures

### Weekly SI: The Beck Depression Inventory (BDI)

The BDI (Beck, Ward, Mendelsohn, Mock, & Erbaugh, 1961) is a 21-item self-report measure that assesses depression symptom severity. Each item is rated from 0 (no disturbance) to 3 (maximal disturbance). Item scores are summed for a total severity score. The suicide item is rated on a scale from 0 (“I don’t have any thoughts of killing myself”) to 3 (“I would kill myself if I had the chance”) point Likert scale. The BDI has good internal consistency (α = .89) and good reliability and validity in adolescents (Roberts, Lewinsohn, & Seeley, 1991).

## Measures

### Weekly PTSD Measure: The Child PTSD Symptom Scale – Self Report (CPSS-SR)

The CPSS-SR (Foa et al., 2001) is the self-report version of the CPSS-I. Each item is rated on a 0 (not at all) to 3 (very much) point Likert scale (range: 0 – 51). The CPSS-SR has excellent psychometric properties (Foa et al., 2013). Agreement between the CPSS-SR and CPSS-I was excellent (85.5%), and one-week test-retest reliability assessed for CPSS-SR scores was excellent (r = .86; Gillihan, Aderka, Conklin, Capaldi & Foa, 2013).

## Treatments

### Prolonged Exposure for Adolescents (PE-A; Foa et al., 2008)

PE-A was adapted from the PE therapy manual (Foa, Hembree, & Rothbaum, 2007) and was delivered over 14 sessions and three treatment phases. The first phase offered psychoeducation and treatment planning. The second phase included presentation of the in vivo and imaginal exposure rationale, construction of an in vivo exposure hierarchy, and imaginal and in vivo exposure practice. The third phase included generalizing skills, relapse prevention, and completing a final project on treatment gains.

## Treatments

### Client Centered Therapy (CCT; Cohen & Mannarino, 1996)

CCT focused on establishing a trusting relationship with the treatment provider. The structure of treatment was based on concepts of betrayal, stigmatization and powerlessness. The sessions were participant-directed, except for sessions 4 and 8 when participants were asked to answer one question about their feelings regarding their trauma. Counselors provided active listening, reflection, and empathy.

## Procedure

This study received approval from the institutional review board at the University of Pennsylvania. After potential participants called the crisis center’s hotline, they completed an initial screening with a counselor who assessed for sexual abuse and length of time since trauma.

Female, adolescent, participants and their non-offending primary guardian signed informed assent/consent forms and completed a 2- to 3-hr baseline eligibility evaluation. Eligible participants completed 1–3 preparatory sessions to address case management issues. If imminent suicide risk was identified, participants were excluded from the study and referred elsewhere for treatment. After the pretreatment sessions, participants were randomized to PE-A or CCT using a parallel design and a permuted block procedure with 10 randomizations per block (1:1 ratio). Participants received up to 14, 60- to 90-min sessions of PE-A or CCT depending on developmental level, attention span, and specific clinical needs. Participants completed the CPSS-SR and the BDI prior to each treatment session and at all masked independent evaluations, which occurred before treatment, at mid-and post-treatment, and at 3-, 6-, and 12- month follow-up. Participants were compensated $40 for completing all assessments except the post-treatment assessments, for which participants were compensated $60.

## Data Analytic Plan

All analyses used an intent to treat sample. A one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to determine whether there were any baseline differences in SI (as measured by the suicide item on the BDI) by Condition. Mixed effects longitudinal models were explored given the multiple observations of SI nested within participants with maximum likelihood estimation.1 The intraclass correlation (ICC) of an empty model (i.e., a model without any predictors) determined that the inclusion of random intercepts was warranted (ICC = .58).

Time was centered at post-treatment and explored as a linear, quadratic, and piecewise effect (Time during treatment and Time in follow-up modeled separately) using Akaike’s Information Criterion (AIC) and Bayes Information Criterion (BIC). A test of linear Time (AIC: 1037.38, BIC: 1067.75), quadratic Time (AIC: 1009.49, BIC: 1044.93), and piecewise Time (AIC: 886.12, BIC: 936.75) revealed that piecewise Time resulted in the best model fit for SI. Random effects of Time improved model fit (χ2 = 98.19, p < .0001) and were retained.

An unstructured covariance matrix was used along with robust standard errors. Once the trajectory of SI change over Time was determined, a main effect of Condition (PE-A vs. CCT) and a Time × Condition interaction was added to the model. Effect sizes for the Time × Condition interaction were calculated following recommendations from prior literature (Feingold, 2013, equation 9), wherein the coefficient of the interaction was multiplied by the duration of Time and divided by the pooled standard deviation for the non-reference group (in this case, PE-A). Effect sizes for the Time × Condition interaction were calculated following recommendations from prior literature (Feingold, 2013, equation 9), wherein the coefficient of the interaction was multiplied by the duration of Time and divided by the pooled standard deviation for the non-reference group (in this case, PE-A). Effect sizes for main effects were calculated by dividing the coefficient by the pooled standard deviation, consistent with recommendations from prior literature (Feingold, 2013, equation 3).

Next, we sought to determine whether the slope of PTSD severity (measured by the CPSS-SR) during treatment was associated with the slope of reductions in SI during treatment. Thus, we calculated a mixed effects model of the change in CPSS-SR over each treatment session and extracted random intercepts and slopes from this model for each participant. These random intercepts and slopes were entered into a mixed effects model with SI as the outcome and Time during treatment, slope of CPSS-SR in treatment, Time in treatment × Slope of CPSS-SR in treatment interactions, and intercept of CPSS-SR in treatment as predictors. Then, a three-way Time in treatment × Slope of CPSS-SR in treatment × Condition interaction was added to the model along with a main effect of Condition to determine whether the relationship between PTSD slope in treatment and SI slope in treatment was moderated by Condition. This was repeated after extracting the slope of CPSS-SR in follow-up, which was then entered into a mixed effects model with SI as the outcome and Time during follow-up, slope of CPSS-SR in follow-up, Time in follow-up × Slope of CPSS-SR in follow-up interactions, and intercept of CPSS-SR in follow-up as predictors. Then, a three-way Time in follow-up × Slope of CPSS-SR in follow-up × Condition interaction was added to the model along with a main effect of Condition to determine whether the relationship between PTSD slope in follow-up and SI slope in follow-up was moderated by Condition.

There was not a significant difference in baseline SI by Condition (p = .15). Time was modeled with two segments representing Time in treatment and in follow-up. Across all participants, there was a significant reduction in SI in treatment (see Table 2, Model 1), but no change in SI in follow-up.

The Time in treatment × Condition interaction on SI was significant (d = 0.52; see Table 2, Model 2), suggesting that PE-A had a steeper decline in SI relative to CCT (see Figure 2). The Time in follow-up × Condition interaction was not significant (p = .785, d = 0.09).

The Time in treatment × CPSS-SR slope in treatment was significant (d = 0.26; see Table 2, Model 3). Greater reduction in CPSS-SR in treatment was associated with a significantly steeper reduction in SI in treatment. When Condition was added to the model, the Time in treatment × CPSS-SR slope in treatment × Condition interaction was not significant (p = .221). The Time in follow-up × CPSS-SR slope in follow-up interaction was significant (d = 0.54, see Table 2 Model 4). Greater reduction in CPSS-SR during follow-up was associated with significantly steeper reduction in SI in follow-up. When Condition was added to the model, the Time in follow-up × CPSS-SR slope in follow-up × Condition interaction was not significant (p = .691).

The Time in treatment × BDI slope in treatment interaction was significant (d =.18, see Table 2, Model 5). Greater reduction in BDI during treatment was associated with a significantly steeper reduction in SI in treatment. When Condition was added to the model, the Time in treatment × BDI slope in treatment × Condition interaction was not significant (p = .396). The Time in follow-up × BDI slope in follow-up interaction was significant (d =.82, see Table 2 Model 6). Greater reduction in BDI during follow-up was associated with significantly steeper reduction in SI in follow-up. When Condition was added to the model, the Time in follow-up × BDI slope in follow-up × Condition interaction was not significant (p = .114).

When both the BDI (p = .522) and CPSS-SR (p = .160) were entered into a model to simultaneously predict change in SI in treatment, neither was significant. In contrast, when both the BDI (coefficient: 0.043, 95% CI: 0.011, 0.074, SE: 0.016, z = 2.67, p < .01, d = .34) and CPSS-SR (coefficient: .059, 95% CI: 0.002, 0.116, SE = .029, z = 2.02, p < .05, d = .26) were entered into a model to simultaneously predict change in SI in follow-up, both were significant.

PE-A was associated with significantly steeper reductions in a single-item measure of SI from pre- to post-treatment relative to CCT. While participants were not recruited on the basis of SI, across both conditions there was a significant reduction in SI, though this effect was driven by PE-A, which was associated with faster gains relative to CCT. This finding suggests the relative safety of trauma-focused treatments with adolescents in relation to SI. No differences between PE-A and CCT emerged at post-treatment or in the follow-up period, and there was not a significant change in SI over follow-up.

Consistent with prior research (Bryan et al., 2016; Gradus et al., 2013), the slope of PTSD and depression symptom reduction was associated with the degree of reduction in SI. However, this effect was not moderated by condition. Regardless of whether an adolescent with PTSD received PE-A or CCT, the degree of SI improvement was associated with the degree of PTSD and depression symptom improvement. These findings provide further justification for initiating PTSD treatment rather than waiting for SI to resolve. As PTSD and depression symptoms resolve, suicide ideation will likely also resolve. When depression and PTSD were added in a simultaneous model, neither predicted the change in SI in treatment, but both predicted the change in SI in follow-up. Therefore, both depression and PTSD improvements may drive reduction in SI over time, though their predictive power in treatment is reduced when both are included in a simultaneous model.

As noted earlier, adolescents are at higher risk for PTSD (Mclaughlin et al., 2013) and SI (Centers for Disease Control & Prevention, 2015) relative to adults. To our knowledge, no prior research has reported on the reduction in SI in response to trauma-focused treatments for adolescents. This knowledge gap may be largely due to clinical recommendations to avoid trauma-focused treatments until SI resolves (Van Minnen et al., 2015). However, for many individuals with PTSD, trauma-related distress and depression symptoms drive thoughts of suicide (Bryan et al., 2016; Gradus et al., 2013). For these individuals, suicide ideation may not resolve without treatment that ameliorates PTSD and depression symptoms. PE may have an advantage in that the reduction of PTSD and depression is faster and thus relief from SI occurs faster.

Several mechanisms may underlie improved SI in PE-A and CCT. As mentioned earlier, interpersonal relationships are strongly associated with SI (Finkelhor et al., 2009) and PE is associated with significant improvements in relationship quality (Foa et al., 2005). In addition, a primary treatment target in CCT is on improving the relationship with the therapist (Foa et al., 2013). Perhaps treatment improved the quality of interpersonal relationships, which consequently reduced thoughts about suicide. Alternatively, treatment may have improved the perception of relationship quality, which reduced feelings of loneliness and decreased SI. Indeed, improvement in trauma-related negative cognitions about others is associated with significant improvement in PTSD symptoms in adolescents (McLean, Yeh, Rosenfield & Foa, 2015). Another possible mediator of SI change is improvement in nightmares. PE and CCT significantly reduce nightmare frequency (Brownlow, McLean, Gehrman, Harb, Ross & Foa, 2016), and nightmares are strongly predictive of SI (Nadorff, Nazem, & Fiske, 2011). These associations were not explicitly explored in this paper as this would require the addition of several additional analyses, which would increase the risk of Type I error. However, these potential mediators should be explored in future, larger samples.

Several limitations of this study should be considered. A single-item measure of SI was used in the current study, which reduces confidence in the results. Nevertheless, two studies have confirmed that the BDI suicide item is predictive of future suicidal thoughts and behavior (Farabaugh et al., 2015; Green, Brown, Jager-Hyman, Cha, Steer & Beck, 2015), and therefore this single-item measure is of value for exploratory work in this novel area. Consistent with the relatively high rates of SI in adolescents (Mclaughlin et al., 2013), approximately 40% of the sample reported some degree of SI at baseline. However, the range of SI in this sample was small, and the sample was not severely suicidal (means ranged from 0.10 to 0.58 on a 0- to 3-point scale). Therefore, the results from the current study should be replicated with a sample explicitly recruited on the basis of suicide risk. The current study did not include a measure of suicide attempts. Future research should examine the change in suicide attempts in adolescents. Additionally, although the sample was diverse in race/ethnicity, participants were all female and experienced the same index trauma (sexual assault), limiting the extent to which the results can be generalized males or to females with alternative index traumas. Finally, the study sample size was modest; the study should be replicated in a larger sample, and a larger sample may reveal significant differences in the follow-up period, where results were non-significant.

Clinically, this study demonstrates that adolescents with mild to moderate SI may benefit from PE-A. For some individuals, SI may be considered a secondary symptom of PTSD that is unlikely to resolve until PTSD is treated. In individuals with more severe SI, PTSD treatment augmentation may be necessary to ensure safety. Future research should evaluate the efficacy of integrating trauma-focused treatments with brief suicide-focused interventions for adolescents with more severe suicide risk.

In summary, this is the first study to examine the changes in SI in trauma-focused treatments for adolescents. PE-A was associated with significantly faster improvements in SI compared to CCT. Reduction in PTSD and depression were each associated with reduction in SI over time. Therefore, for patients with mild to moderate SI, PE-A is a safe and effective option for treating both PTSD and SI.
Funding: This work was supported by the National Institutes of Mental Health under Grant [R01 MH074505].

review_title: Comparative effectiveness of psychological interventions for treating the psychological consequences of sexual abuse in children and adolescents: a network meta‐analysis
